paper_title: Measuring the Depth of LLM Unlearning via Activation Patching

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large language model (LLM) unlearning has emerged as a crucial post-hoc mechanism for privacy protection and AI safety, yet auditing whether target knowledge is truly erased remains challenging. Existing output-level metrics fail to detect when this knowledge remains recoverable from internal representations. Recent white-box studies reveal such residual knowledge but often rely on auxiliary training or dataset-specific adaptations, leaving no generalizable metric. To address these limitations, we propose the Unlearning Depth Score (UDS), a metric that quantifies the mechanistic depth of unlearning via activation patching. UDS first identifies layers that encode the target knowledge using a retain model baseline, then measures how much of it is erased in the unlearned model on a 0-1 scale. In a meta-evaluation across 20 metrics on 150 unlearned models spanning 8 methods, UDS achieves the highest faithfulness and robustness, confirming our causal approach as the most reliable for unlearning evaluation. Case studies further reveal that white-box metrics can disagree at the layer level and that erasure depth varies across examples. We provide guidelines for integrating UDS into existing benchmarking frameworks and streamlining the evaluation pipeline. Code and data are available at https://github.com/gnueaj/unlearning-depth-score

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Jaeung Lee, Dohyun Kim, Jaemin Jo* Sungkyunkwan University Republic of Korea {dlwodnd00, kimdoh0423, jmjo}@skku.edu 6 2 0 2 3 2 ] . [ 1 4 1 6 4 2 . 5 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large language model (LLM) unlearning has emerged as crucial post-hoc mechanism for privacy protection and AI safety, yet auditing whether target knowledge is truly erased remains challenging. Existing output-level metrics fail to detect when this knowledge remains recoverable from internal representations. Recent white-box studies reveal such residual knowledge but often rely on auxiliary training or dataset-specific adaptations, leaving no generalizable metric. To address these limitations, we propose the UNLEARNING DEPTH SCORE (UDS), metric that quantifies the mechanistic depth of unlearning via activation patching. UDS first identifies layers that encode the target knowledge using retain model baseline, then measures how much of it is erased in the unlearned model on 01 scale. In metaevaluation across 20 metrics on 150 unlearned models spanning 8 methods, UDS achieves the highest faithfulness and robustness, confirming our causal approach as the most reliable for unlearning evaluation. Case studies further reveal that white-box metrics can disagree at the layer level and that erasure depth varies across examples. We provide guidelines for integrating UDS into existing benchmarking frameworks and streamlining the evaluation pipeline.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs) memorize substantial portions of their training data (Tirumala et al., 2022), posing risks to privacy and AI safety when such data includes sensitive personal information or hazardous knowledge (Carlini et al., 2021; Bengio et al., 2025). LLM unlearning addresses this by removing target knowledge from trained model while preserving its general capabilities (Jang et al., 2023). The goal is to produce model indistinguishable from one trained entirely without the target *Corresponding author 1Code and data are available at https://github.com/ gnueaj/unlearning-depth-score 1 data (Bourtoule et al., 2021), and growing body of methods now pursues this objective (e.g., Jang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024). Yet, fundamental question remains: how do we verify that knowledge has been genuinely removed? Recent benchmarking efforts have sought to systematically evaluate existing methods (Maini et al., 2024; Shi et al., 2025; Li et al., 2024) and visually compare them (Lee et al., 2026), advocating that reliable metric should exhibit faithfulness (accuracy in detecting knowledge) and robustness (stability under interventions) (Dorna et al., 2025). While these benchmarking frameworks rely primarily on output-based metrics, adversaries can restore ostensibly erased knowledge through lightweight fine-tuning (Fan et al., 2025a) or activation manipulation (Seyitoglu et al., 2024; Jang et al., 2026). This vulnerability suggests that evaluation must move beyond output logits, leading several studies to explore white-box analyses to identify residual knowledge (Hong et al., 2024; Lynch et al., 2024; Guo et al., 2025). However, current whitebox approaches often require auxiliary training or are tied to specific datasets, leaving no generalizable score for systematic method comparison. To address these limitations, we propose the UNLEARNING DEPTH SCORE (UDS), training-free, causal, and dataset-invariant metric that quantifies the mechanistic depth of unlearning via activation patching. We define depth as how far unlearning penetrates the models internals, rather than merely altering final outputs. UDS operates in two stages: (1) baselining stage that identifies knowledgeencoding layers by patching hidden states from the retain model (i.e., trained without target data) into the full model (i.e., trained on all data including the target), and (2) quantification stage that patches the unlearned models hidden states into the full model to measure how much of the encoded knowledge persists. Unlike prior diagnostic analyses, UDS causally intervenes to test whether the knowledge is recoverable, yielding per-example score from 0 (intact) to 1 (erased) that reflects the erasure depth across layers. In our comprehensive meta-evaluation of 20 metrics on 150 unlearned models spanning 8 methods, UDS achieves the highest faithfulness (AUC-ROC 0.971) and robustness (HM 0.932), outperforming both output-level metrics and white-box baselines. Our case studies demonstrate that causal evaluation uncovers residual knowledge obscured by representational shifts that mislead observational metrics, and that erasure depth varies across examples within single method. Finally, we provide guidelines for integrating UDS into existing frameworks and streamlining the evaluation pipeline. To summarize, our contributions are: UNLEARNING DEPTH SCORE (UDS), metric that quantifies the mechanistic depth of unlearning via two-stage activation patching. meta-evaluation of 20 metrics on 150 unlearned models over 8 methods, demonstrating that our causal approach most reliably evaluates knowledge erasure. Case studies uncovering residual knowledge obscured by representational shifts that mislead observational metrics, with per-example analysis and guidelines for integrating UDS into existing benchmarking frameworks.

sections.title: 2 Background and Related Work
sections.content: 2.1 LLM Unlearning Given trained model, forget set Df , and retain set Dr, the goal of machine unlearning is to produce model indistinguishable from one trained only on Dr (Bourtoule et al., 2021), preserving general capabilities (Yao et al., 2024). Methods. The simplest approach, gradient ascent (Jang et al., 2023), maximizes loss on Df , but unconstrained optimization leads to catastrophic collapse. GradDiff (Yao et al., 2024; Maini et al., 2024) mitigates this by jointly minimizing loss on Dr, though balancing opposing gradients remains fragile. NPO (Zhang et al., 2024) reframes this tension through preference optimization, treating forget set completions as dispreferred, and SimNPO (Fan et al., 2025b) simplifies this by removing the reference model and normalizing by response length. IdkNLL and IdkDPO (Maini et al., 2024) instead train the model to produce alternative responses (e.g., dont know), and AltPO (Mekala et al., 2025) extends this with in-domain positive feedback on plausible alternatives. RMU (Li et al., 2024) intervenes at the representation level, misdirecting hidden states toward random targets, while UNDIAL (Dong et al., 2025) uses self-distillation on adjusted logits to steer output distributions away from Df . Models unlearned with these methods across hyperparameter sweeps form the evaluation pool for our metric comparison in 4. Evaluation Frameworks. Unlearning is typically evaluated along three axes: memorization, whether the model can still reproduce forget set knowledge; privacy, whether an adversary can detect that the model was trained on Df ; and utility, whether general performance is preserved. Benchmarks such as TOFU (Maini et al., 2024), MUSE (Shi et al., 2025), and WMDP (Li et al., 2024) addressed these concerns, and OpenUnlearning (Dorna et al., 2025) consolidated them into unified framework. OpenUnlearning also introduced meta-evaluation to assess metric reliability itself by two criteria: faithfulness, whether the metric can distinguish models with vs. without forget set knowledge, and robustness, whether it remains stable under interventions like quantization and finetuning. We extend this framework by introducing symmetric robustness criterion. Unlike the original formulation that solely penalizes knowledge recovery, our symmetric formulation evenly evaluates instability in either direction (see 4.1). 2.2 White-box Evaluation of LLM Unlearning Beyond output-level evaluation, variety of techniques can probe model internals. CKA (Kornblith et al., 2019) compares representational geometry across layers, Logit Lens (nostalgebraist, 2020) decodes intermediate hidden states through the models prediction head, Fisher Information (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017) quantifies parameter sensitivity to specific data, and activation patching (Meng et al., 2022) causally tests knowledge by patching hidden states between models. Applying these techniques to unlearning, several studies have shown that seemingly unlearned models preserve forget set knowledge internally. Xu et al. (2025) use CKA and Fisher diagnostics to characterize reversibility of unlearning across layers. Lynch et al. (2024) train probes on hidden states to detect latent knowledge invisible to output metrics. Guo et al. (2025) use causal tracing to localize factual recall circuits, then confirm residual 2 Work Train-Free Causal Data-Inv Score Lynch et al. (2024) Guo et al. (2025) Hong et al. (2025) Patil et al. (2024) Hong et al. (2024) UDS (Ours) Table 1: Comparison of white-box unlearning analysis. Train-Free: no auxiliary training. Causal: evaluates knowledge causally ( = causal localization but observational evaluation). Data-Inv: directly applicable to new forget sets. Score: proposes metric quantifying residual forget set knowledge. knowledge with trained probes. Hong et al. (2025) project MLP value vectors into vocabulary space to show that parametric knowledge traces persist after unlearning. Patil et al. (2024) apply logit lens projections to demonstrate that intermediate layers still decode supposedly erased knowledge. Hong et al. (2024) use parameter restoration to show that finetuning-based unlearning modifies MLP coefficient scores in the final layers without altering the underlying value vectors, leaving stored knowledge intact. These studies reveal residual knowledge, but they are primarily diagnostic: none provides standardized, comparable score that generalizes across forget sets  (Table 1)  . UDS addresses this with training-free, causal, dataset-invariant score for systematic method comparison (see 3).

sections.title: 3 The UNLEARNING DEPTH SCORE
sections.content: In this section, we describe UDS, metric that quantifies the depth of unlearning by measuring how recoverable forget set knowledge is via activation patching (Figure 1). We define the problem setup (3.1), describe the patching procedure (3.2), discuss an efficient implementation strategy (3.3), and validate the metric across model scales (3.4). Appendix provides further ablations to validate our design choices. 3.1 Problem Setup consider Terminology. We three models: (i) Mfull, trained on the full dataset Dr Df ; (ii) Mret, trained only on Dr (the gold standard for unlearning); and (iii) Munl, obtained by applying an unlearning method to Mfull. Following Ghandeharioun et al. (2024), we call the model whose hidden states are extracted the source and the model that receives them the target. 3 Input and Measurement. Each forget set example consists of an input context xi and an entity span yi = (yi,1, . . . , yi,Ti). We focus on entity spans because common template phrases are predictable regardless of knowledge retention. To avoid generation noise and enable stable per-token comparison across models, we use teacher forcing: the full input sequence is fed in single forward pass. The model predicts each entity token yi,t conditioned on the ground-truth prefix yi,<t. In autoregressive language models, the hidden state at position predicts token p+1; we therefore examine positions yi,11 through yi,Ti1. 3.2 Two-Stage Activation Patching UDS proceeds in two stages: Stage 1 patches Mrets hidden states into Mfull to establish baseline, and Stage 2 replaces the source with Munl to quantify erasure. We first run Mfull on each example to obtain reference log-probabilities sfull i,t . Mfull serves as the target at each stage because it has learned forget set knowledge and can decode it from the patched hidden states. Stage 1: Baselining. To establish baseline of knowledge unique to the forget set, for each example and layer l, we patch the source Mrets residual stream (see Appendix D.1 for componentlevel analysis) into Mfull at the positions where entity tokens are predicted. We then measure the degradation in log-probability: S1 i,l = 1 Ti Ti(cid:88) t=1 (cid:0)sfull i,t sS1 i,t (cid:1) (1) where sS1 i,t denotes the corresponding value for entity token yi,t after patching layer l. large S1 i,l indicates that Mfull encodes forget set knowledge for example at layer that Mret lacks. Layers with negligible S1 i,l reflect noise rather than knowledge encoding, so we set threshold τ and keep only the Knowledge-Encoding (KE) layers (see Appendix D.2 for sensitivity analysis): KEi = {l : S1 i,l > τ }, τ = 0. (2) This also bounds the denominator S1 Erasure Ratio (Eq. 4) away from zero. i,l in the Layer Stage 2: Quantification. We repeat the same procedure with Munl as the source to quantify how much of this knowledge remains recoverable: Ti(cid:88) (cid:0)sfull i,t sS2 i,t (cid:1) (3) S2 i,l = 1 Ti t= Figure 1: Overview of UDS for single forget set example. (A) Stage 1 patches hidden states from Mret into Mfull at each layer to measure how deeply the forget set knowledge is encoded. (B) Stage 2 repeats this with Munl as source to quantify how much encoded knowledge remains recoverable. (C) Stage 2 degradation is compared against Stage 1 at each layer to compute erasure ratios, which are weighted and aggregated into single 01 score. If unlearning erased the knowledge for example at layer l, patching Munl should degrade predictions as much as patching Mret: S2 i,l . Conversely, if the knowledge remains intact, Mfull can still decode it from the patched states, so S2 i,l 0. i,l S1 Score Aggregation. We define the Layer Erasure Ratio (LER) to represent each layers erasure as fraction of its baseline: baselines S1 i,l , and the KE layer sets can be computed once and cached. Evaluating any subsequent unlearned model requires only extracting its hidden states and running the Stage 2 patched forward passes. Furthermore, leveraging teacher forcing across pre-identified entity spans computes all token predictions in single forward pass per layer, avoiding the latency of autoregressive generation. (cid:33) 3.4 Validation Across Model Scales LERi,l = clip (cid:32) S2 i,l S1 i,l , 0, 1 (4) where clipping to [0, 1] ensures that the metric caps at the target unlearning level defined by Mret. The per-example UDS aggregates LER across KE layers, weighted by S1 i,l so that layers encoding more forget set knowledge contribute proportionally: UDSi = (cid:80) lKEi (cid:80) S1 i,l LERi,l S1 i,l lKEi (5) score of 1 indicates knowledge erased to the level of Mret, while 0 indicates fully intact knowledge. If KEi = , the score is undefined and the example is excluded from aggregation. The model-level score averages over the remaining examples: UDS = 1 (cid:88) i=1 UDSi (6) 3.3 Efficient Implementation To efficiently evaluate large pools of unlearned models, UDS uses the following implementation strategy. Because Stage 1 depends solely on Mret and Mfull, the reference log-probabilities sfull i,t , the To verify that UDS reliably captures unlearning depth regardless of model scale, we evaluate it across Llama 1B, 3B, and 8B (Grattafiori et al., 2024) using TOFU (Maini et al., 2024) retain splits as source models. Source Model 1B 3B 8B full (0% unseen) retain99 (10% unseen) retain95 (50% unseen) retain90 (100% unseen) 0.002 0.153 0.496 1.000 0.008 0.151 0.482 1.000 0.000 0.101 0.455 1.000 Table 2: UDS across Llama 1B, 3B, and 8B. S1 baseline is retain90 at each scale. As shown in Table 2, the monotonic ordering full < retain99 < retain95 < retain90 holds at all three scales, with UDS values proportional to the fraction of the forget set each model has not seen. Values decrease slightly with scale, which is expected since larger models have greater capacity and thus small difference in training data causes less representational shift. For instance, removing 1% of training data perturbs the hidden states of an 8B model less than those of 1B model, resulting in smaller UDS. These results confirm that the monotonicity and proportionality of UDS remain consistent across scales. Figure 2: Quantization test for Truth Ratio and ROUGE. Truth Ratio dots lie along the diagonal, indicating stable scores; ROUGE dots in the blue box fall below the diagonal. One-directional formulas do not penalize this decline, but our symmetric formula (Eq. 8) does.

sections.title: 4 Meta-Evaluation
sections.content: To validate UDS, we adopt and extend the metaevaluation framework of Dorna et al. (2025), which tests metric faithfulness and robustness. 4.1 Setup Models and Dataset. We evaluate on the TOFU forget10 benchmark (Maini et al., 2024) using the Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct (Grattafiori et al., 2024) architecture. The faithfulness evaluation uses Ppool (30 models trained on data including Df ) and an N-pool (30 models trained without Df ). The robustness evaluation spans 150 unlearned models produced by 8 methods described in 2 across hyperparameter sweeps (see Appendix for details). Mfull and Mret serve as reference models. Comparison Metrics. We compare UDS against 19 metrics. Twelve are from Dorna et al. (2025): eight memorization metrics (ES, EM, Prob, ParaProb, Truth Ratio, ROUGE, Para-ROUGE, Jailbreak-ROUGE) and four MIA variants (LOSS, ZLib, Min-K, Min-K++). Since UDS is retain-referenced and operates on internal representations, we add four retainreferenced MIA variants and three white-box baselines. The retain-referenced MIA variants (sLOSS, sZLib, sMin-K, sMin-K++) scale raw MIA AUC against Mret, adapting PrivLeak normalization (Shi et al., 2025): Figure 3: Faithfulness evaluation of four white-box metrics via P/N pool separation. CKA measures geometry and Fisher measures gradient sensitivity, neither of which directly reflects knowledge content, yielding poor separation. Logit Lens and UDS distinguish the pools well; UDS further benefits from causal intervention. (frozen-decoder readout), and Fisher (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017) (parameter sensitivity; top 0.1% mask). Formal definitions appear in Appendix B. Evaluation Protocol. To assess faithfulness, we compute the AUC-ROC to measure how well each metric separates the P-pool from the N-pool. For robustness, we evaluate metric stability under 4-bit quantization and 1-epoch relearning on Df . Dorna et al. (2025) score quantization stability as min(m/m, 1) and relearning stability as min(ret/unl, 1), where (m) is the metric value before (after) the intervention, oriented so that higher values indicate knowledge retention, and = m. These asymmetric formulations penalize knowledge recovery (i.e., score increases) but overlook spurious metric degradation (e.g., score drops caused by impaired generation). For instance, quantization can degrade generation quality by reducing model precision, causing ROUGE to decline regardless of knowledge content (Figure 2, blue box), yet such decline is rewarded by construction. We therefore propose symmetric alternatives to penalize changes in both directions: = 1 min (cid:18) AUCm AUCret AUCret (cid:19) , 1 (7) = 1 m + , = 1 unl ret unl + ret (8) The white-box baselines compare Munl and Mret at each layer, aggregated as (cid:80) wl where wl captures layer importance and el measures erasure: CKA (Kornblith et al., 2019) (representational similarity), Logit Lens (nostalgebraist, 2020) wl el / (cid:80) In practice, small constant is added to the denominators to prevent division by zero. For the normalized MIA scores and the four white-box metrics, where higher values indicate erasure, we apply 1m prior to computation. 5 Group Metric Overall Faith. Agg. Quant. Relearn Scope Robustness Memorization Extraction Strength Exact Memorization Probability Paraphrased Probability Truth Ratio Generation ROUGE Paraphrased ROUGE Jailbreak ROUGE Privacy Normalized White-box MIA-LOSS MIA-ZLib MIA-Min-K MIA-Min-K++ sLOSS sZLib sMin-K sMin-K++ CKA Fisher (Masked 0.1%) Logit Lens UDS (Ours) 0.875 0.782 0.786 0.782 0.542 0.456 0.209 0.438 0.767 0.737 0.774 0.677 0.778 0.790 0.786 0.686 0.051 0.716 0.902 0. 0.891 0.817 0.816 0.707 0.947 0.722 0.832 0.757 0.902 0.867 0.907 0.816 0.891 0.870 0.891 0.799 0.648 0.712 0.927 0.971 0.859 0.750 0.757 0.875 0. 0.333 0.119 0.308 0.668 0.641 0.675 0.579 0.690 0.724 0.704 0.602 0.026 0.721 0.879 0.932 0.970 0.984 0.924 0.853 0.996 0.934 0.951 0. 0.935 0.938 0.923 0.883 0.719 0.704 0.710 0.643 0.997 0.583 0.959 0.968 0.770 0.605 0.642 0.899 0.234 0.203 0.064 0.183 0.519 0.487 0.532 0. 0.663 0.745 0.697 0.566 0.013 0.946 0.812 0.900 Table 3: Meta-evaluation of 20 unlearning metrics, with best, second, worst, and second-worst marked in each column. Scope tags indicate whether metric is output-level (O), retain-referenced (R), or internal (I). Each metric is scored on faithfulness (AUC-ROC) and robustness (harmonic mean of quantization and relearning); Overall combines both via harmonic mean. UDS ranks first in Overall, Faithfulness, and Aggregate Robustness. 4.2 Results Following Dorna et al. (2025), we restrict robustness evaluation to models that preserve at least 80% utility of Mfull and are classified as unlearned by the metrics faithfulness threshold. Per-metric robustness is the harmonic mean (HM) of and averaged across the models, and the overall score combines faithfulness and robustness via harmonic mean. Table 3 reports full results for all 20 metrics; per-metric plots appear in Appendix E.2. Faithfulness. UDS achieves the highest faithfulness (AUC 0.971). The second best and top output-level metric is Truth Ratio (0.947), in line with Dorna et al. (2025). Notably, the white-box baselines diverge. CKA (0.648) and Fisher (0.712) achieve poor separation: CKA measures how similarly two models represent the same dataset, but unlearning can alter representational geometry without removing specific knowledge, so low similarity does not entail erasure; Fisher measures gradient sensitivity, which has been shown to reflect optimization trajectories rather than true knowledge content (Basu et al., 2021). Logit Lens (0.927) reads knowledge through the frozen decoder, achieving strong separation; UDS improves upon this through causal intervention (Figure 3). Robustness. UDS ranks first in aggregate robustness (HM = 0.932), with balanced quantization (Q = 0.968) and relearning (R = 0.900) stability. Unlike output metrics that are easily disrupted by output-level distribution shifts, UDS extracts intermediate representations and evaluates them through the unperturbed computational pathways of Mfull. By bypassing the unlearned models output head, UDS is resilient to weight compression. Furthermore, it remains stable under relearning: while single epoch of fine-tuning triggers rapid generation recovery by realigning the output space, it does not substantially alter the deeply encoded knowledge that UDS measures. Logit Lens follows (HM = 0.879), though its fixed-decoder readout is less stable under relearning (R = 0.812). By contrast, CKA collapses under relearning (R = 0.013) because global representational geometry shifts even under brief fine-tuning. Fisher is the most vulnerable to quantization (Q = 0.583), below even the MIA variants (Q0.643, the least quantization-stable among output-level metrics), because weight compression distorts the gradient landscape it relies on. Among output-level metrics, ROUGE variants are highly unstable under relearning (R = 0.060.20): residual knowledge in the unlearned model enables rapid recovery of gener6 Logit Lens UDS (Ours) Prompt Type Example Entity Mean UDS Layer S1 S2 LER S1 S2 LER 04 5 7 9 11 13 15 not KE not KE not KE 0.375 0.250 0.667 0.312 0.812 1.000 0.053 0.059 0.000 0.039 0.113 1.375 1.375 1.000 0.346 0.088 0.105 1.250 2.062 1.000 0.838 0.299 0.230 0.926 2.465 1.000 1.299 0.436 0.254 1.713 0.436 0.254 1.713 Score 0.801 0. Table 4: Layer-wise comparison on forget set example from an IdkDPO model. Logit Lens judges the knowledge as erased (0.801) but UDS does not (0.209); observational decoding misses knowledge that causal intervention identifies as recoverable. ation, far exceeding what the retain model learns for the first time in one epoch. Truth Ratio, despite the second-highest faithfulness, degrades sharply under relearning (R = 0.234). Overall, UDS achieves the highest score (0.951) across all 20 metrics, confirming that the causal approach provides the most reliable evaluation of knowledge erasure.

sections.title: 5 Case Studies
sections.content: UDS provides per-layer, per-example erasure scores, enabling analyses beyond aggregate evaluation. We present two analyses: observational vs. causal evaluation (5.1), and prompt-type variation in erasure depth within single model (5.2). 5.1 Observational vs. Causal Evaluation As analyzed in 4.2, the causal approach of UDS achieves higher faithfulness than the observational readout of Logit Lens. We examine specific forget set example to understand the mechanism driving this discrepancy. Because Logit Lens relies on frozen decoder, it is vulnerable to representational shifts: if an unlearning method rotates or distorts the internal vector space, the fixed unembedding matrix fails to read the forget set knowledge and falsely concludes it has been erased. UDS overcomes this limitation because its causal patching allows the remaining nonlinear layers of Mfull to actively process and realign these distorted vectors. Table 4 illustrates this gap on an IdkDPO model predicting the entity historical fiction. Logit Lens reports high aggregate erasure score of 0.801, indicating complete erasure (LER = 1.000) from layers 7 through 13. In contrast, UDS yields an overall Yes/No Person Name Book/Title Biographical Descriptive Leading with Creativity 21 Yes 15 Hsiao Yun-Hwa 88 Artistic Authority: 75 dietician; 2002; Seoul 162 cultural understanding, inclusivity and diversity Leadership Literary Award Overall 361 0.624 0.025 0.038 0.049 0.042 0.076 Table 5: Per-prompt-type UDS from single IdkNLL model. Yes/No questions show substantially higher erasure (0.624) than all other types (0.0250.049), revealing that erasure depth varies by prompt type. score of 0.209, revealing that the forget set knowledge remains highly recoverable (LER 0.230) across these mid-layers. Both metrics converge to an identical Layer Erasure Ratio (0.254) at the final layer, where representations directly determine the output logits. This layer-wise divergence demonstrates that while representational shifts easily mislead observational metrics, UDS confirms the underlying knowledge remains intact. 5.2 Heterogeneity of Unlearning Depth Even within single unlearning method, unlearning depth varies substantially across examples. We illustrate this with IdkNLL, which unlearns by replacing correct answers with dont know. All configurations score approximately 0.0 on every normalized MIA variant, yet UDS differentiates them, ranging from 0.039 to 0.253. Table 5 reports per-prompt-type scores for one model: Yes/No questions reach 0.624, while all other types (e.g., person names, book titles) fall below 0.049. For Yes/No questions, dont know functions as negation of the factual answer, modifying deeper KE layers. For other prompt types, this response bears no semantic relation to the original entity, and only the output distribution changes. Per-example UDS enables identifying which knowledge types given method fails to erase internally.

sections.title: 6 Practical Implications
sections.content: We discuss two practical implications of UDS: integrating it into the privacy evaluation axis (6.1), and streamlining the evaluation pipeline (6.2). 6.1 Integrating UDS into the Privacy Axis Existing frameworks evaluate the privacy axis primarily through output-level MIA metrics (Shi et al., 7 Method w/o (Rank) w/ (Rank) MIAagg UDS Best by w/o UDS Best by w/ UDS AltPO SimNPO NPO IdkDPO GradDiff RMU UNDIAL IdkNLL 0.784 (1) 0.733 (3) 0.752 (2) 0.720 (4) 0.686 (5) 0.631 (6) 0.088 (7) 0.000 (8) 0.766 (1) 0.722 (32) 0.710 (23) 0.709 (4) 0.637 (5) 0.625 (6) 0.103 (7) 0.000 (8) 0.952 0.816 0.875 0.757 0.789 0.711 0.091 0. 0.816 0.739 0.619 0.686 0.515 0.667 0.871 0.251 Table 6: Method ranking before and after integrating UDS into the privacy axis. Each score is HM(Memorization, Privacy, Utility); see Appendix B.4 for axis definitions. Best configuration per method selected by the w/o formula. w/o: Privacy = MIAagg; w/: Privacy = HM(MIAagg, UDS). Adding UDS swaps NPO and SimNPO, exposing internal erasure differences that MIAagg alone misses. 2025; Jin et al., 2024; Dorna et al., 2025); for instance, MUSE defines Privacy = sMin-K. However, the demonstrated risk of latent knowledge extraction (Lynch et al., 2024) highlights the need for broader definition. We suggest extending this by defining: Privacy = HM(MIAagg, UDS) (9) where MIAagg is the harmonic mean of four MIA variants from Dorna et al. (2025), each normalized against Mret (Eq. 7). This harmonic formulation penalizes one-sided degradation, encouraging deeper internal erasure while maintaining output-level privacy. Impact on Method Ranking. Table 6 compares method rankings before and after integrating UDS into the privacy axis. Adding UDS to the privacy axis causes NPO and SimNPO to swap ranks (23). NPOs best configuration, selected without UDS, achieves high MIAagg score (0.875) but moderate internal erasure (UDS = 0.619). Conversely, the SimNPO configuration yields lower MIAagg score but higher UDS (0.739); its length-normalized, reference-free objective drives unlearning pressure deeper into intermediate representations. While establishing fundamental algorithmic superiority requires broader investigation, this configuration-level rank swap illustrates how UDS complements existing evaluations by exposing residual internal knowledge that output-only metrics overlook. Impact on Hyperparameter Selection. UDS also reshapes which configurations practitioners Method LR α Epoch LR α Epoch UDS AltPO NPO 5e-5 1 2e-5 5 10 5e-5 2 5e-5 5 10 10 +0.016 +0.199 Table 7: Two configuration shifts under the w/ UDS formula. α is the retention coefficient. Both methods shift toward higher learning rates or longer unlearning, improving UDS. would select. Table 7 shows two examples: when reselecting the best configuration with UDS formula, both AltPO and NPO shift toward higher learning rates or longer training. These shifts demonstrate that integrating UDS steers hyperparameter selection toward deeper internal erasure. 6.2 Streamlining the Evaluation Pipeline Under current evaluation frameworks, verifying the robustness of unlearning typically requires applying perturbations such as quantization and relearning to each model before re-running the evaluation suite. Repeating this process across large pool of models creates substantial computational overhead. In our meta-evaluation (4), UDS demonstrates high stability under both quantization and relearning, which drives its highest aggregate robustness. This stability makes the pre-perturbation UDS score strong predictor of robust knowledge erasure under deployment perturbations, allowing practitioners to skip exhaustive post-perturbation benchmarking and streamline the overall evaluation pipeline.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We present UNLEARNING DEPTH SCORE (UDS), metric that quantifies the mechanistic depth of unlearning by measuring knowledge recoverability through two-stage activation patching. UDS produces 01 erasure scores, complementing outputonly metrics with causal intervention. In our metaevaluation, UDS achieved the highest faithfulness and robustness among all 20 metrics, demonstrating both reliable knowledge detection and stability under deployment perturbations. Our case studies show that UDS captures knowledge retention invisible to output-level metrics, and its robustness makes it practical substitute for costly postperturbation evaluation pipelines.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Requirement of Retain Model. For rigorous quantification, UDS employs two-stage patching, which requires retain model. While UDS remains useful for method development, internal audits, and benchmark curation, such model may not be available in all deployment contexts. Without retain model, practitioners can utilize the quantification stage alone, patching hidden states from the unlearned model into the full model (i.e., the original model prior to unlearning). Although this omits retain-based normalization, observing the degradation in prediction probability still serves as causal indicator of residual knowledge. Clipping and Over-Unlearning. The clip(, 0, 1) operation in Eq. 4 caps UDS at 1.0, so overunlearning (representations deviating beyond the retain model) is mathematically indistinguishable from perfect unlearning. Practitioners should jointly monitor UDS with the utility axis (i.e., the preservation of general capabilities) to diagnose such cases. Dataset. To maintain methodological alignment with the meta-evaluation framework of Dorna et al. (2025), our current evaluation focuses on the TOFU benchmark (Maini et al., 2024). Validating UDS on other unlearning benchmarks (e.g., Shi et al., 2025; Li et al., 2024) would further strengthen its generality across diverse domains. Entity Span. UDS currently operates on localized entity spans under teacher forcing. How it extends to long-form or multi-step reasoning targets beyond factoid entities remains an open question. Our automatic extraction pipeline (Appendix C) handles structured QA pairs but may not generalize to all data formats.

sections.title: Broader Impact
sections.content: UDS enables the detection of incomplete unlearning, supporting responsible AI deployment and regulatory compliance efforts. However, to avoid false sense of security, UDS should be deployed as part of comprehensive safety pipeline rather than viewed as an absolute guarantee of complete data removal. The two-stage activation patching framework underlying UDS is not specific to autoregressive language models. Any architecture with layered representations (e.g., diffusion models, vision transformers) could in principle be audited for residual knowledge through analogous patching procedures. Adapting the concrete metric formulation to these settings is an open direction for future work.

sections.title: A Unlearning Details
sections.content: We evaluate 150 unlearned models produced by 8 methods, each trained on the TOFU forget10 split (Maini et al., 2024) using Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct (Grattafiori et al., 2024). Below we report each methods training objective as implemented in Open-Unlearning (Dorna et al., 2025); Table 8 lists the hyperparameter grid. Several methods below build on the DPO objective (Rafailov et al., 2023): LDPO(yw yl; θ) = (cid:18) log σ β log πθ(ywx) πref(ywx) β log (cid:19) πθ(ylx) πref(ylx) GradDiff (Yao et al., 2024; Maini et al., 2024). Combines gradient ascent on Df with standard cross-entropy on Dr: = LNLL(Df ; θ) + α LNLL(Dr; θ) NPO (Zhang et al., 2024). Treats forget set completions as dispreferred responses using the losing term of LDPO, with β controlling the strength of the penalty relative to reference model πref: = 2 β EDf (cid:104) log σ (cid:16) (cid:17)(cid:105) β log πθ(yx) πref(yx) + α LNLL(Dr; θ) SimNPO (Fan et al., 2025b). Removes the reference model from NPO and normalizes by response 11 Method Learning Rate Swept Fixed Epoch Models GradDiff (Yao et al., 2024) NPO (Zhang et al., 2024) SimNPO (Fan et al., 2025b) IdkNLL (Maini et al., 2024) IdkDPO (Maini et al., 2024) AltPO (Mekala et al., 2025) RMU (Li et al., 2024) UNDIAL (Dong et al., 2025) Total {1e-5, 2e-5, 5e-5} {1e-5, 1e-4, 3e-4} α {1, 2, 5} β = 0.1 α {1, 2, 5} α {1, 2, 5} β {3.5, 4.5}, γ {0.125, 0.25} α = 1 α {1, 2, 5} α {1, 2, 5} α {1, 2, 5} {5, 10, 15} β = 0.1 β = 0.1 = 10 γ = 10 5, 10 18 18 24 18 18 18 18 18 Table 8: Hyperparameter grid for all 8 unlearning methods, yielding 150 models. length, with δ as reward margin and γ weighting the forget loss: = 2γ β EDf (cid:104) log σ (cid:16) β (cid:16) (cid:17)(cid:17)(cid:105) 1 log πθ(yx) δ + α LNLL(Dr; θ) IdkNLL (Maini et al., 2024). Fine-tunes on refusal answers (I dont know) for forget set questions, where Didk replaces the original answers with refusal responses: = LNLL(Didk ; θ) + α LNLL(Dr; θ) IdkDPO (Maini et al., 2024). Applies the DPO objective with the refusal response as preferred and the original answer as dispreferred: = LDPO(yidk yf ; θ) + α LNLL(Dr; θ) AltPO (Mekala et al., 2025). Extends IdkDPO by generating in-domain alternate answers via temperature sampling as preferred responses (M =1 in our experiments): = 1 (cid:88) i=1 LDPO(yi yf ; θ) + α LNLL(Dr; θ) RMU (Li et al., 2024). Operates in representation space, misdirecting hidden states at designated layer toward random target cu while anchoring retain representations to the original model θ0. Gradients are applied only to layers l2, l1, l. (cid:104) = EDf h(l) θ (x) cu2(cid:105) (cid:104) h(l) + α EDr θ (x) h(l) θ UNDIAL (Dong et al., 2025). Suppresses the ground-truth token logit by β in the frozen reference models output, then distills the adjusted distribution into the student model: = EDf (cid:104) 1 (cid:88) CE(cid:0)softmax(zt β eyt), πθ(x, y<t)(cid:1)(cid:105) + α LNLL(Dr; θ)

sections.title: B Metric Definitions
sections.content: B.1 Output-Level Metrics Extraction Strength (ES). Fraction of the answer extractable via greedy decoding (Carlini et al., 2021): ES = 1 k/T where is the answer length in tokens and is the earliest position at which the generated continuation matches the ground-truth suffix. Exact Memorization (EM). Token-level position match ratio (Tirumala et al., 2022): EM = 1 (cid:88) t=1 (ˆyt = yt) Probability (Prob). Geometric mean of pertoken probabilities under teacher forcing: Prob = exp( 1 x, y<t). t=1 Lt), where Lt = log pθ(yt (cid:80)T Paraphrase Probability (ParaProb). Geometric mean of Prob computed across paraphrased answer variants provided in the TOFU dataset. Truth Ratio. Normalized correct vs. incorrect probability (Maini et al., 2024): Truth Ratio = pc pc + pw , (cid:32) = exp (cid:33) Lt 1 T (cid:88) t=1 (x)2(cid:105) where pc and pw are computed over the correct and perturbed (incorrect) answers, respectively. 12 ROUGE. ROUGE-L recall between model generation (greedy, max 200 tokens) and the groundtruth answer. Para. ROUGE. Same as ROUGE, but computed against the paraphrased answer reference provided in TOFU. Jailbreak ROUGE. Same as ROUGE, but the assistant turn is prefixed with Sure, here is the answer: to bypass refusal behavior. MIA Metrics. Four membership inference attack scoring functions, each computing per-sample scores that are aggregated into an AUC over member/non-member classification: (cid:80) cross-entropy 1 MIA-LOSS (Yeom et al., 2018): average Lt MIA-ZLib (Carlini et al., 2021): loss normalized by zlib compression length of the answer, ℓ/zlib(y) MIA-Min-K (Shi et al., 2024): mean of bottom-k% log-probs (k=0.4) MIA-Min-K++ (Zhang et al., 2025): standardized Min-K using per-position z-scores B.2 Retain-Referenced and White-Box Metrics Normalized MIA (s). Each raw MIA AUC is rescaled against the retain models AUC (Eq. 7): normalized = AUCmodel AUCret AUCret = 1 min(normalized, 1) yielding four variants: sLOSS, sZLib, sMin-K, sMin-K++. Higher indicates less deviation from retain (i.e., more erased). CKA (Centered Kernel Alignment). Measures representational geometry similarity between models (Kornblith et al., 2019). Let Cfr = CKA(Hfull, Hret)l and Cur = CKA(Hunl, Hret)l. Per-layer erasure is: erasurel = clip (cid:18) Cur Cfr + ϵ 1 Cfr (cid:19) , 0, 1 The final score is (cid:80) wl = 1 Cfr . wl erasurel / (cid:80) wl, where Logit Lens. Projects each layers hidden states through the full models frozen decoder to measure decodable knowledge (nostalgebraist, 2020). 13 Let km,l be the mean log-probability of entity tokens when decoding through the full models decoder, and dm,l = kfull,l km,l. In particular, dS1,l = kfull,l kret,l is the S1 baseline gap. Perlayer erasure is: erasurel = clip(dm,l/dS1,l, 0, 1) score final The erasurel / (cid:80) (analogous to KE in UDS; 3). l: dS1,l>τ dS1,l, where τ = 0.05 l: dS1,l>τ dS1,l is (cid:80) Fisher Masked. Diagonal Fisher Information (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017) with top-p% parameter masking per layer. For each layer, the perparameter anchor importance is ai = max(Fret,i Ffull,i, 0), and Ml selects the top p% of parameters by ai. Let Fm,l = mean(Fm[Ml]) and el = Fret,l Ffull,l. The per-layer erasure is: (cid:18) Fret,l Funl,l el + ϵ wl erasurel / (cid:80) erasurel = 1 clip wl with , 0, 1 The final score is (cid:80) wl = el. (cid:19) B.3 Mask Fraction Sensitivity Table 9 reports Fisher Masked faithfulness and robustness across three mask fractions; = 0.1% is used as the representative fraction. Mask Faithfulness HM(Q, R) 0.01% 0.1% 1% 0.708 0.712 0.698 0.573 0.583 0.582 0.945 0.946 0.940 0.713 0.721 0.719 Table 9: Fisher Masked across mask fractions: faithfulness (AUC-ROC) and symmetric robustness (Q: quantization, R: relearning). B.4 Score Computation for Method Ranking Following Dorna et al. (2025), each score in Table 6 is HM(Memorization, Privacy, Utilityrel): Memorization = HM(1ES, 1EM, 1ParaProb, 1Truth Ratio) Utility = HM(MU, Fluency) where MU (Model Utility) is the harmonic mean of nine QA metrics (Prob, ROUGE-L, Truth Ratio) evaluated on three TOFU subsets (Retain, Real Authors, World Facts), and Fluency is the nongibberish rate of generations on forget set questions. Utilityrel = Utility/Utilityfull normalizes against Mfull. In Table 6, Privacy is either MIAagg alone (w/o) or HM(MIAagg, UDS) (w/).

sections.title: C Dataset Details
sections.content: For each TOFU forget10 benchmark with 400 forget set QA pairs, we partition the answer into prefix and target entity span without modifying the original text. To ensure systematic extraction, we prompt GPT-5.2 to output the exact character index that separates the contextual prefix (e.g., the sequence immediately following the main verb or copula) from the target factual entity. Table 10 shows representative examples of these partitions. Type Prefix Entity Yes/No Person Biograph. Genre Descript. Yes (empty) The authors full name is Hsiao Yun-Hwa ...Ji-Yeon Park was born in ...predominantly writes in the genre of ...often themes of incorporating Historical Fiction Seoul, South Korea diversity and inclusion Table 10: Prompt type examples with prefix and entity.

sections.title: D UDS Ablation Studies
sections.content: D.1 Component Patching Each Llama transformer layer computes: al = Attn(RMSNorm(hl1)) ml = hl1 + al hl = ml + MLP(RMSNorm(ml)) (residual + attention) Figure 4: Mean S1 patching delta per layer for four patching locations. Patching the full layer output hl (used by UDS) captures the dominant share of the knowledge, with the gap widening in later layers. Shaded regions denote 1 standard deviation. We select τ =0.05 as the default: the number of skipped examples remains stable at 6 (1.5%) from τ =0.03 to 0.05, then jumps to 14 (3.5%) at τ =0.10. The threshold stabilizes the denominator of the UDS formula and filters noise from low-delta layers. D.3 Entity Length Bias We check whether entity token length biases UDS. Figure 5 shows weak correlations (ρ < 0.24) with mixed sign across methods, indicating no consistent directional bias. Meta-Evaluation Details (layer output) E.1 Robustness Attack Settings We test four patching locations to determine which component carries the knowledge signal. Figure 4 reports the mean S1 delta per layer. As shown in Figure 4, the full layer output hl produces the largest delta across all layers. UDS therefore patches the residual stream hl by default. D.2 KE Threshold Sensitivity The KE layer set is determined by S1 deltas (retain full). Table 11 reports how the KE set changes across six τ values. τ Mean KE Std Skipped % Skipped 0.00 0.01 0.02 0.03 0.05 0.10 14.4 2.7 13.2 3.2 12.4 3.3 11.8 3.3 10.9 3.3 9.6 3. 0 2 3 6 6 14 0.0% 0.5% 0.8% 1.5% 1.5% 3.5% Table 11: S1 KE layer set size across threshold values. Skipped examples have no layer with S1 > τ . Quantization. BitsAndBytes NF4 quantization with bfloat16 compute dtype (load_in_4bit=True). No additional calibration or fine-tuning is applied after quantization. 4-bit Relearning. One epoch of fine-tuning on Df with the following settings: learning rate = 2 105, batch size = 8, gradient accumulation = 4 (effective batch size = 32), optimizer = AdamW. E.2 Full Per-Metric Plots We provide per-metric scatter plots for both robustness attacks. Figure 6 shows quantization robustness (Q) and Figure 8 shows relearning robustness (R), both after applying the utility filter ( 0.8) and faithfulness filter (4.1). Figure 7 shows quantization with utility filtering only, where metrics such as ES and ROUGE exhibit score drops across many models. White-box metrics (CKA, Fisher, Logit Lens, UDS) are plotted as 1 score so that higher values correspond to more knowledge, consistent with the output-based metrics. 14 Figure 5: Per-example UDS vs. entity token count (lr=2e-5, epoch 10; UNDIAL uses lr=1e-4). RMU variants differ by the target layer at which the steering loss is applied (L5, L10, L15). All methods show ρ < 0.24 with mixed sign, indicating no consistent directional bias. Figure 6: Quantization robustness (Q) for all 20 metrics after utility and faithfulness filtering. Each subplot plots the metric value before (x) vs. after (y) NF4 4-bit quantization, with the number of models showing recovery (rec) or destruction (des). is the number of models that passed both filters for that metric. The background gradient indicates deviation from the = reference: white = stable, red = unstable. 16 Figure 7: Quantization robustness (Q) for all 20 metrics after utility filtering only. Each subplot plots the metric value before (x) vs. after (y) NF4 4-bit quantization, with the number of models showing recovery (rec) or destruction (des). is the number of models that passed the utility filter for that metric. The background gradient indicates deviation from the = reference: white = stable, red = unstable. Metrics such as Extraction Strength and ROUGE show score drops across many models after quantization. 17 Figure 8: Relearning robustness (R) for all 20 metrics after utility and faithfulness filtering. Each subplot plots the metric value before (x) vs. after (y) one epoch of relearning, with the number of models showing over-recovery (over) or under-recovery (under). is the number of models that passed both filters for that metric. The dashed line shows = + ret (expected behavior given the retain models shift); the dotted line shows = x. The background gradient indicates deviation from the expected line: white = stable, red = unstable. The red star marks the retain model.
